chapters.title: A lovable Saint Bernard learns life lessons in the frozen north
chapters.text:
Call of The Wild follows four-and-a-half-year-old Buck, a huge Saint Bernard and half Scottish Shepherd dog. Living a charmed life in Santa Clara Valley, California, with his owner Judge Miller, Buck spends time with Molly and Alice, Judge’s daughters, with whom he enjoys long walks and plenty of fun.

A cruel twist of fate leads Buck away from his home and thrusts him into the heart of the action in the frozen lands of Alaska, at the heart of the Klondike Gold Rush. Forced to work grueling, long hours and often beaten by master after master, Buck is forced to realize that life is far from charming.

Call of The Wild may seem like another animal adventure story on the outside, but on the inside, it is about learning some of life’s harshest and most challenging lessons. Buck sees things he never thought existed and experiences emotions and pain that no dog should ever feel. In the end, he learns life’s greatest lesson — that no matter how much you run, no matter how much you try to deny it, your true self, your true life’s calling will catch up with you in the end.

Always be yourself — just like Buck, you’ll find that sticking to your true identity always gets you through.

There is much to learn from Buck’s journey, and while it is an emotional one indeed, the novel’s deeper themes touch every person’s journey through life. Whether living a charmed life, a problematic existence, or you’re not sure where you stand, the hardships and lessons that Buck endures will open your eyes to things we should all understand — that love, devotion, loyalty, and being kind to others are the cornerstones of life.

There is an excellent reason why Call of the Wild has been so wildly popular over the years and continues to be to this very day. It isn’t about unwavering love of animals; it’s about the deeper themes and the affection you will undoubtedly feel for Buck when you pick up the novel.

chapters.title: Buck is quickly forced to realize that life isn’t charmed for long
chapters.text:
Buck is content with his lot. He is a lovable companion to Molly and Alice, and to his master Judge Miller.

Buck, Molly, and Alice would often go on long walks in the countryside, and always alert and ready to pounce, Buck was willing to protect his two young friends with his life.

The whole realm was his. He plunged into the swimming tank or went hunting with the Judge’s sons; he escorted Mollie and Alice, the Judge’s daughters, on long twilight or early morning rambles. ~ Jack London

Buck was happy, but little did he know things were about to change because far north in the frozen lands of Alaska, the importance of gold was beginning to flourish. Men from all over the country were leaving their lives behind and heading north, hoping to find gold and never have to work again. Judge Miller wasn’t one of those men; happy to be living at home with his two girls and his faithful dog, Miller had no intention of going anywhere. However, Manuel, one of his servants, had other ideas.

Wracked with gambling debt, Manuel steals Buck under cover of darkness and sells him to a group of criminals who supply gold traders in the north with dogs. Because of Buck’s size and his undeniable strength, he was a true asset to any trader, and it didn’t take long before he was on his way to the frozen lands of the north to be part of the famous Klondike Gold Rush.

Sometimes in life, we must follow the rules just to get through. But that doesn't mean you can’t start to make a plan for the future.

Sold to a new master and forced to drag tortuously heavy sleds through the frozen snow, Buck quickly realizes that home is very far away, and he must obey to survive. His first master, the Man in the Red Sweater, beats Buck and mistreats him, and every escape attempt is foiled.

Did you know? Call of the Wild has been translated into more than 30 languages.

chapters.title: Buck begins to learn harsh lessons as the truth inside starts to awaken
chapters.text:
Beaten and forced to work grueling hours, Buck starts to learn the harshness of the situation he has found himself in. With little food or water, he is pushed to his limits, dragging sleds and learning to work as part of a team of dogs. He burrows holes into the snow to sleep, although he rarely manages to get more than an hour or two.

Buck lives in a constant state of hunger, and these extreme circumstances force him into survival mode. He starts to rely on his intelligence, gut feelings, and instincts to know when to comply, when to try something new, and how to get by.

Despite all of this, Buck notices that his instincts are far more carnal and deep-rooted than he ever imagined. He experiences a pull so strong it’s almost irresistible, and he quickly learns that he needs to be on high alert at all times to stay alive. Always ready to pounce, always prepared to fight and jump onto the offensive, Buck leaves behind his former charmed life and happy sentiments and starts to take on an altogether more adjusted and somewhat darker mind-frame. He learns to adapt.

Living in constant pain and never trusting anyone, Buck realizes that his size is an asset and the other dogs in the pack fear him. However, he quickly befriends Curly, a large but friendly Newfoundland. It is through Curly that Buck remembers who he is.

Good friends help ground us and pull us back to who we really are. Who are your closest, genuine friends?

chapters.title: Forced to fight the alpha, Buck becomes the dominant male in the pack
chapters.text:
It is through Curly that Buck learns his first lesson of loss. The alpha dog in the pack, Spitz, cruelly kills Curly and rekindles Buck’s desire for blood. Due to his extreme hunger and the pull toward instincts he is constantly trying to push back down, Buck finds that the power takes over when his masters force him to fight Spitz.

Far larger in stature and fueled by his hunger, Buck kills Spitz, and because of his efforts, earns the rest of the pack’s respect. He becomes leader and alpha while also winning the admiration of his current masters Francois and Perrault.

During this period, Buck’s masters are somewhat kinder than the others he has experienced. But this doesn’t last long as Francois and Perrault are called away on business, and Buck is, again, sold. His new owner, a half Scotsman, is fair and relatively kind but forces Buck to work extremely hard and pushes him into more than adverse conditions. Buck is exhausted, mentally tortured, and loses an extreme amount of weight.

During this time, Buck also loses some other pack members due to the harsh living conditions. Buck survives due to his natural strength and survival instincts that he is learning to heed more and more.

Your gut, or your intuition, will always guide you through difficult times in life. Practice listening to yours.

chapters.title: Buck meets a man who wins his devotion and, ultimately, his heart
chapters.text:
Once more, Buck and the rest of his pack are sold, this time to the haphazard and unprofessional trio of Charles, Hal, and Mercedes. Being from the south, Charles completely underestimates the conditions in the north, and is clueless about how to handle dogs of this size. Hal, Mercedes’ brother, is a cruel man who beats Buck regularly and always carries a gun, knife, and a whip.

The inexperience of the trio causes the pack’s food supply to dry up far faster than it should, leaving the dogs starving and significantly lacking in energy. Driven almost insane by hunger and frustration, Buck refuses to comply with demands, leading him to be badly beaten. At this time, a mystery man named John Thornton appears on the scene.

But love that was feverish and burning, that was adoration, that was madness, it had taken John Thornton to arouse. This man had saved his life, which was something; but, further, he was the ideal master. ~ Jack London

John is a kind and helpful man, and upon seeing Buck's beaten state, he threatens the trio with violence if he sees the dog being hit once more. Buck instantly warms to Thornton, and his devotion is solidified when Charles, Mercedes, and Hal drown while trying to cross the ice. Sadly, many of the other dogs die too.

Thornton suffers the effects of frostbite, but while trying to recuperate, he also tends to Buck and nurses him back to health. Buck feels the impact of kindness and love, and as a result, he devotes his time and attention to protecting Thornton and remaining by his side. This loyalty is tested, yet Buck never wavers.

During these far more idyllic times for Buck, he sometimes ventures out into the forest during the evening when he hears a mysterious call, something that seems to be guiding him back “home.” The more he follows this call, the more he feels his instincts strengthen — a pull towards the wild and a desire to be tethered no more.

No matter how far you try to run from your home, you’ll always be pulled back in some way. The call is never far away.

chapters.title: Disaster strikes and forces Buck’s instincts to be unleashed in fury
chapters.text:
Buck is Thornton’s truest protector, always by his side and looking out for danger. He is there to save Thornton whenever anyone threatens or attacks him, and he does this many times.

However, Thornton has big ideas and, like most men, has one eye on hitting the big time. During a successful gambling session, he wins $1600 and, with his partners Hans and Pete, heads into the wilderness, where there are rumors of a lost gold mine. This gives Buck even more opportunities to wander into the trees when the men are resting, where he finds a pack of wild wolves. The calling he has been feeling comes back even more strongly when he is with the wolves, and he often spends time running with them.

He heads back to camp one particular day after spending time with the wolves, and disaster has struck; Thornton and his partners have been killed by a fierce tribe of Indians called the Yeehaw. Blinded by fury and rage, Buck attacks the Yeehaws and kills many of their tribe, driving the rest far away. The remaining Yeehaws vow never to return to that spot, considering it a demonic place.

Having lost his master and devoted leader, Buck finally gives in to the calling and heads into the wilderness for the last time, where he joins forces with the wolves. He becomes the leader of a new pack of wild dogs running in the area, extending across the entire northern regions. Buck howls the “song of the pack,” a calling that reaches out to every hound in the area, telling them to listen to their calling. The extreme emotions force Buck to face his instincts and see himself as his reflection for the first time. It is a freeing feeling and one he no longer resists.

Plagued by the grief of losing his master, Buck vows to listen to his true self for the rest of his days and dedicates his time to the wilderness, never to be forced to drag sleighs or heavyweights across impacted ice and snow ever again.

You cannot live your life freely without being true to who you are.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Call of the Wild has several lessons to learn, but it is also a very emotional and deep tale that connects with the reader on a far greater level than most novels. Perhaps telling the story through an animal’s eyes is the most significant difference and works exceptionally well.

Call of the Wild isn’t just a story; it’s not a tale of a dog done well; it’s an emotional journey that teaches us to listen to our instincts and be our true selves every single day.

Buck could never run from who he was; he simply veered from disaster to disaster, no matter how hard he tried to steer the reins in a different direction. He connected emotionally with several characters in the book, from his original owner, the Judge, and his daughters, to old faithful Curly, who succumbed to the fierce Spitz, leading to an epic battle that proved to be the turning point of the book.

When Buck kills Spitz, he opens Pandora’s box of ability. He learns that he can be both fierce and devoted, and protecting those he loves is worth fighting for and worth killing for. He understands that coming into his power gives him inner strength and confidence.

Call of the Wild doesn’t only teach us to embrace our true selves; it also teaches us about morals and transformation throughout Buck’s emotional journey. That’s certainly a lesson we could all learn.

Try this

1. Who are your genuine friends? It’s time to do a friend inventory and learn who is true to you.

2. Is there anything in your life that you do out of duty that you really don’t like or feel suits your inner voice? It’s time to cut unnecessary things out of your life.

3. Do you often have a pull towards doing something new? Spend time researching new routes to explore.

Read it till the end?
